event: The Angel Raphael Instructing Adam in the Garden of Eden
symbols: angel pointing upward — directing Adam's attention to the heavenly Creator, the source of all things
lush garden setting — the paradisal abundance of Eden before the Fall
Adam seated and attentive — the first man receiving instruction about the nature of God and creation
angel's wings — the heavenly origin and authority of the messenger
dense foliage and flowers — the perfection and fertility of unfallen creation
description: A detailed black-and-white engraving depicting two figures seated together in an extraordinarily lush garden setting. On the left, a nude man sits on a rock, his body muscular and idealized, looking attentively at his companion. On the right, a winged figure in flowing robes sits beside him, one hand raised and pointing upward, as if teaching or explaining something. The garden around them is dense with tropical vegetation — enormous palm fronds, broad-leafed plants, flowering bushes, and towering trees form a canopy overhead.
theological_description: This illustration from Milton's Paradise Lost depicts the archangel Raphael's visit to Adam in the Garden of Eden, but it draws directly on the biblical account of humanity's original state. In Genesis 2:15-17, God places Adam in the Garden and gives him instruction — commanding him to tend it and warning him about the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. The angel's upward-pointing gesture directs Adam to the one Almighty from whom all things proceed, echoing the fundamental biblical affirmation of monotheistic creation: 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth' (Genesis 1:1). The paradisal setting captures Genesis 2:8-9, where God planted a garden in Eden with every tree pleasant to sight and good for food. Adam's attentive, receptive posture embodies the original human vocation: to receive divine revelation with an open and obedient heart. This is humanity before the Fall — naked and unashamed (Genesis 2:25), in direct communion with the heavenly realm, dwelling in the abundance of God's provision. The dense, perfect vegetation surrounding them visualizes the goodness of creation that God declared 'very good' (Genesis 1:31). The intimacy of the scene — angel and man in conversation — reflects the biblical ideal of the creature living in transparent relationship with the Creator, an intimacy that will be shattered by disobedience but ultimately restored through Christ, the second Adam (Romans 5:14-19).

scripture_refs.reason: God's instruction to Adam in the Garden, including the command about the tree, is the theological foundation of this teaching scene
The lush garden of Eden planted by God with every beautiful tree is directly depicted in the setting
The angel's upward gesture teaches Adam about the one Almighty Creator from whom all things proceed
Adam's unashamed nakedness reflects the pre-Fall innocence of humanity
Adam as a type of the one to come — the original instruction in Eden foreshadows the fuller revelation in Christ

_meta.title: To whom the winged Hierarch replied O Adam, one Almighty is, from whom All things proceed
